transcript.text: Okay, England. England. This is my travel story for England. England was the first country that I traveled to. I really didn't do any travel in college. I wasn't one of those guys who did the exchange.

The only traveling I've done, I mean, I've been in Mexico for a little bit when I was in college for like a day. I went there for 10 days when I was 10 with my dad and my brother, and that's it. I stepped across the border into Canada once for like five seconds. That was it.

So England, that was exciting to be in another country. I flew to England three days after Princess Diana died, and I got there on a one-way ticket. The lady's like, what's she doing here on a one-way ticket? I'm like, well, I'm just going to bike to her.

She's like, you're not going to work here, are you? And I'm like, no. She's like, you promised. I'm like, what? No. So she lets me through, and I think it was like night time. It was like six or seven at night. The sun had gone down, and I stepped out of Heathrow on my bicycle, and all I had was a mountain bike with shocks.
Not a good idea to bike to her with shocks, but anyways, I had shocks. And I had a rear rack that connects to the seat post, so just a simple rack.
And then I had like a camelback backpack, like bungee cord to the top of that. And on top of that, I had a sleeping bag. So that kind of tight tightly on the back.
And then on the front, I had like a box kind of bag like this. You just snap on, and then I could take it off, you know, and sling it over my shoulder.

I actually bought the sling strap there in England, because I didn't have it yet. So that, you know, when I got off my bike and locked it up, I could put on my camelback, and I could sling my sleeping bag over my shoulder, and then I could sling my little front box bag over my other shoulder, and I could walk around.
You know, and so I was totally portable. It's cool. Bike touring really light is really cool, because you have freedom to do whatever you want.
So anyway, I roll into England, or Heathrow, and I go like 100 meters outside of the gate.
You know, right outside the gate, 100 meters from where I stepped outside of the Heathrow, which is like right in the middle of London, I felt like.
It was right in the city. But it was at night, so I look over towards the north, and I see this kind of patch of woods between the road,
Where there's this gas station here on the left, and then like apartment buildings.
So I go in there, and then there's this ditch, kind of like a drainage ditch, so I sleep in the bottom of it.
So that was pretty cool, because I'm right in the center of town, and I'm like invisible.
It's like having those Harry Potter blanket on. Nobody can see me if they look over, because I'm in the ditch.
And I didn't have a tent with me. I was like, this is my sleeping bag with a baby sack.
And so I sleep there. And I think that night, I went into the gas station and bought like some food,
And I saw, I heard these like kids talking Cockney, and I was like really trippy. Culture shock for me to hear kids speaking Cockney.
Like I don't, I majored in English, and I'd only heard people speak Cockney in like some like movies and stuff, you know.
Like being an American, you're used to English people speaking King's English.
So the next day I get up at like sunrise. It's great camping out, because you get up and the sun comes up,
Because you overheat if you stay in the sleeping bag. So it's cool, because I'm usually asleep in, so it's great that I...
I got up right at sunrise, and then I ride north. And you know, I was right in town, and it's pretty crazy riding on the left side of the road.
And I ride through London, I go through like some kind of pedestrian mall area, and I see some guy dressed up like in one of these old English outfit.
Like some like street performer guy, I was kind of culture shock. And I go through, you know, I go like across the, you know, old bin,
Like the, what's it called, London Bridge, and then through the downtown center, and there's all these like narrow roads.
Which is trippy, because we don't have those in America. They're all curving and stuff, because I'm used to grid streets.
And I go, and I got lost, like, and there's all these roundabouts too, that was cool, we don't have those.
And I got lost, I like, because I remember I saw a roundabout, and then I ride, and then I rode past that same roundabout.
I was like, oh my god. So then I rode north, I just rode through London.
And then I got out of London, and I thought I was out of London, and then there's another town.
And then I think I'm like, oh, okay, that must be like one of the outskirts of London.
And then I hit another town, and then I realized, oh shit, this country is like one big town.
I'm never going to get out of London. I'm always going to be a town-by-town.
And I'm going to have to find a place to camp out, like, in a town, basically.
So I went, like, right outside of some town, next to the road, down the hill a little bit, and camped out.
And I had my radio, and I listen to a lot of radio, and all the radio was about Princess Diana.
Everybody's, and all the newspapers, everybody's talking about it.
Like, everybody's, like, boycotting the game, like, soccer games.
I was only in England a whole week, not one week, but the whole time I was just hearing all this, like, about Princess Diana.
And I go into a mall, and I got some kind of food, and then I picked up a packet of ketchup, and she's like, charge me for it.
And I was like, what, you charge extra for ketchup?
That was pretty wild, because we don't do that in America.
Anyway, I ride north, and it was cool to ride north.
I went through, like, Shakespeare's town, and I would stop at, like, bars, and have, like, a beer or two.
It was kind of, like, exciting for me to get a little bit of a buzz in another country.
But people weren't really interested in, like, talking to me.
I kind of felt like everybody was sad, and so maybe everybody generally was sad, because Princess Diana died.
I remember when the Queen had her speech, but they were like, she was going to have her speech, and then I forgot to, like, tune into it, so I missed it.
And then they, like, I heard them, like, critiquing the speech after.
And I rode through some town.
I think it was, like, Northampton.
I'm just riding down the road, and I see all these people dressed in these, like, cops dressed in black, and all these other people dressed in black.
And I ask this cop, what's going on?
What's all these people lining the street for, dressed in black?
She's like, Lady Diana, Princess of Wales, died.
Going to drive for here.
I'm like, oh, really?
Oh, cool.
Wait on, on a couple hours.
So I go, like, next to M6 on this road, and I just wait there for, like, two hours, and all these people park in some, like, field.
And then I see on M6 just everybody's totally lined on the whole interstate or the whole highway.
And then, sure enough, the hearse drives by, and I throw a flower onto the top of the hearse and take a photo of it.
And then I get on my bike, and I have to go north.
And it was cool going north, because it would just get more and more sparsely populated and hillier and less trees.
And then I crossed into Scotland for, like, three hours.
I ran through Scotland, and, like, the Scottish, they'd look at you and they'd go.
So I guess in Scotland doing this means, like, hi, or something like that.
But that was pretty.
And then I went down, and I toured Hadrian's Wall.
And it looked like it was built yesterday.
They built it really well.
And you could see it for as far as I could see it.
Right now it's only, like, four feet high.
But I went to, like, one of the bastions they had there.
And then I went south, and I went up, like, the steepest road I'd ever been on in my life.
And then down some big hill was, like, the only one.
Oh, but before that I went on some really beautiful hills, like, really green.
All these, like, walls made out of bricks and, like, all the cabins and houses, even roofs,
Are made out of, like, not bricks, but, like, stones.
So it's, like, hundreds of years of stonework collecting stones off of them, or thousands of years off of the fields.
So the fields are totally beautiful and green, and, like, all these stone work everywhere.
It was really beautiful, and hills.
And anyway, I go into Hull.
In the two weeks, in the one week I was there, this one people at a bar offered them to let me stay at their place in a bed they had.
And then this other lady offered to let me stay in her, like, garage.
But then I went into Hull, because I took the boat from Hull into Netherlands, and I was excited about being in Hull because my great-grandmother was from Hull.
And I got some fish and chips.
That was the first time I got fish and chips.
That's, like, I guess, an English food there.
It was cool eating the English food there because I liked it.
I guess people like to talk trash about it, but I like how they have, like, fried tomatoes.
And then, like, bacon and eggs and all that.
It's just, I mean, it's really rich food and will make you fat if you don't exercise.
But if you're riding your bike all day long, it's a perfect diet.
And, um, but I did notice it was more expensive, though.
It was, like, eight bucks for a plate than America.
But, um, so then I got on the boat, and I went into, uh, Netherlands.
And I don't even recall them even looking at my passport.
I just jump on, like, a ferry and tie my bike up, and I lay on, like, a couch.
And then I saw Mr. Bean on the ferry going over into Holland, and I was excited.
And I was like, yeah, another country.
They're not going to speak my language, but...
So that's England!
So thank you for that—

Asheville, I was just a great day.
